description: ISO/IEC 27001:2022 is the international standard for an Information Security Management System (ISMS). Annex A lists 93 reference controls across 4 themes — Organizational, People, Physical, and Technological — which orgs select from (via a Statement of Applicability) based on their own risk assessment.

items: ISO/IEC 27001 — the international standard for an Information Security Management System (ISMS), jointly published by ISO and IEC.
Current edition: **ISO/IEC 27001:2022**, whose Annex A was restructured into 93 controls across 4 themes — down from 114 controls across 14 domains in the 2013 edition.
Unlike SOC 2, ISO 27001 results in a **certification** issued by an accredited certification body, not an attestation report from a CPA firm.

overview.title: Key Differences from Other Compliance Frameworks

items: **ISO 27001** — certifies you have a structured ISMS (policies, risk management process, continual improvement) in place on an ongoing basis.
**SOC 2** — a CPA firm attests that your specific controls were operating effectively over a period (Type 2) or at a point in time (Type 1).

blocks.text: See the [ISO 27001 vs SOC 2 comparison](/frameworks/soc2) on the SOC 2 page for the reverse framing — many companies pursue both, since ISO is the internationally recognized management-system certification and SOC 2 is the U.S.-centric, audit-focused report enterprise buyers ask for directly.

items: **NIST CSF** — a voluntary risk-management framework (Govern/Identify/Protect/Detect/Respond/Recover) with no certification; widely used by U.S. federal-adjacent and critical-infrastructure organizations as a common language for risk.
**ISO 27001** — a certifiable international management-system standard; you either meet the requirements and get certified, or you don't.

blocks.text: ISO 27001 certification is issued by an accredited certification body, valid for 3 years with annual surveillance audits in between. SOC 2 has no "certification" concept at all — it's a report covering a specific period that has to be reissued (re-audited) every cycle, typically annually.

items: **Organizational (A.5)** — 37 controls. Policies, roles, supplier relationships, incident management, business continuity, compliance — everything that doesn't fit neatly into people, physical, or technology.
**People (A.6)** — 8 controls. How employees and contractors handle information day to day: screening, training, remote work, disciplinary process.
**Physical (A.7)** — 14 controls. Protecting facilities, equipment, and media from physical and environmental threats.
**Technological (A.8)** — 34 controls. The largest and most technical theme — endpoint security, access control, cryptography, secure development, network security, logging and monitoring.

blocks.text: Unlike SOC 2's mandatory-vs-optional split, ISO 27001 doesn't pre-select which controls apply to you — your own risk assessment determines which of the 93 are relevant, documented in a **Statement of Applicability (SoA)**. It's normal, and expected, to exclude controls that genuinely don't apply, as long as you can justify why.
See the [Controls tab](/frameworks/iso-27001/controls) for the full list of all 93 controls across the 4 themes.

items: **International recognition** — the global standard for information security management, especially valuable when selling outside the U.S., where SOC 2 is less universally recognized.
**A real management system, not just controls** — ISO 27001 requires an ongoing risk management process and continual improvement, not just a snapshot of controls at one point in time.
**Increasingly required in procurement** — many enterprise and government RFPs outside the U.S. treat ISO 27001 certification as a baseline requirement, not a nice-to-have.
**Customers** — a globally recognized signal that security is managed systematically, not ad hoc.
**Partners/investors** — certification (with the accredited body's name behind it) is easy to verify independently.
**Regulators** — a mature ISMS makes it easier to demonstrate due diligence across multiple jurisdictions' requirements at once.

blocks.text: Quick self-score across all four Annex A themes. If you're answering "no" to more than half the questions in any theme, prioritize that theme's controls first.

rows: Do you have a top-level information security policy with clearly assigned roles?
Do you maintain an up-to-date inventory of information assets with owners assigned?
Is there a formal, documented access control policy tied to business need?
Do you assess and manage security risk with suppliers, including in contracts?
Do you have a documented incident management process, from detection through lessons learned?
Do you have a tested plan for maintaining security during a disruption?
Do you maintain a register of applicable legal, regulatory, and contractual security obligations?
Are new hires screened, and does everyone get regular security awareness training?
Do you have documented security requirements for remote/hybrid work?
Is physical access to secure areas restricted and logged?
Are facilities protected against fire, flood, and other environmental threats?
Is data securely wiped from equipment before disposal or reuse?
Do you enforce least-privilege access with MFA for sensitive systems?
Do you identify and remediate technical vulnerabilities on a defined timeline?
Do you log security-relevant events and actively monitor for anomalies?
Do you have a defined policy for when and how encryption is used?
Do you apply security requirements throughout your development lifecycle?
Are backups automated, tested, and is there redundancy for critical systems?

items: Your risk assessment (not a generic checklist) should be what actually determines which of the 93 controls apply — the table above is a starting point, not a substitute.
[ISO's official standard page](https://www.iso.org/standard/27001) is the canonical source; the standard itself has to be purchased, but ISO also publishes free supporting guidance.

blocks.text: Building the risk assessment and treatment plan properly — and getting management genuinely engaged in reviewing it, not just signing off — is consistently the long pole. After that, technological controls requiring new tooling (centralized logging, vulnerability management, secure development lifecycle) take the longest to operationalize, similar to SOC 2.

items: **A top-level security policy and named roles** — required, cheap, and everything else references back to it.
**An asset inventory** — you can't protect or classify what you haven't inventoried, and most other controls assume this exists.
**MFA and a formal access control policy** — high impact, usually just configuration work in tools you already run.
**A risk assessment methodology** — needed before you can meaningfully complete the Statement of Applicability.

Once those are in place, move to the higher-effort items — business continuity planning, centralized logging/monitoring, and secure development lifecycle — since those need real process maturity, not just documentation.

blocks.text: First-time certification typically takes 6–12 months, depending on how mature your existing security program already is.

items: **Define ISMS scope** — which parts of the organization, systems, and locations the ISMS covers.
**Risk assessment & treatment plan** — identify risks, decide how to treat each one, and select applicable Annex A controls.
**Statement of Applicability (SoA)** — document which of the 93 controls apply, which don't, and why.
**Implement controls** — remediate gaps and stand up the selected controls.
**Internal audit** — test whether the ISMS actually works before the external auditor does.
**Management review** — leadership formally reviews ISMS performance and risk status.
**Certification audit (Stage 1 + Stage 2)** — the accredited certification body reviews documentation, then tests implementation.
**Stage 1** — a documentation review: does your ISMS, risk assessment, and SoA meet the standard's requirements on paper?
**Stage 2** — an implementation audit: does the ISMS actually operate as documented, tested through interviews, sampling, and evidence review.

blocks.text: Select a certification body accredited by a recognized national accreditation body (in the U.S., that's ANAB). Certification is valid for 3 years, with annual surveillance audits to confirm the ISMS is still operating, and a full recertification audit at the end of the cycle.

The most common findings are an incomplete or poorly justified Statement of Applicability, internal audits that weren't actually independent, and management reviews that happened on paper but without real engagement from leadership.
Certification isn't a one-time event — the ISMS has to keep operating, and gets checked annually.

Annual surveillance audits by your certification body, continuous internal audits and management reviews, and updates to the risk assessment and SoA whenever the business or threat landscape changes materially.
A full recertification audit (similar in depth to the original Stage 2) happens every 3 years to renew the certificate.
As you grow, expand the ISMS scope to cover new systems, locations, or business units, and reassess which Annex A controls now apply before they go live.

categories.title: Organizational Controls
categories.summary: Policies, roles, supplier relationships, incident management, business continuity, and compliance — everything that doesn't fit neatly into people, physical, or technology (37 controls).

controls.notes: Maintain a top-level information security policy, reviewed and approved by management, and communicated to all staff.
Define and assign security roles clearly, with an accountable owner for the ISMS.
Split conflicting duties (e.g. requesting vs. approving access) across different people to reduce fraud/error risk.
Management actively supports and enforces security policies, not just signs off on them.
Maintain a documented process and contact list for engaging law enforcement/regulators when needed.
Participate in security forums or threat-sharing communities relevant to your industry.
Actively collect and analyze threat intelligence relevant to your organization, not just react to incidents.
Bake security requirements into every project from the start, not bolted on at the end.

controls.criteria: Inventory of information and other associated assets
controls.notes: Maintain an up-to-date asset inventory (data, hardware, software) with owners assigned.

controls.criteria: Acceptable use of information and other associated assets
controls.notes: Document and communicate rules for how assets and data can be used.
Require employees and contractors to return all assets (devices, badges, data) on termination.
Classify information by sensitivity (e.g. public/internal/confidential) so controls can scale accordingly.
Label information according to its classification so handling rules are obvious.
Have secure, documented procedures for transferring information internally, externally, and with third parties.
Define and enforce a formal access control policy based on business and security requirements.
Manage the full lifecycle of user identities, from creation to deactivation.
Manage credentials (passwords, tokens, keys) securely across their lifecycle.
Provision, review, and revoke access rights based on least privilege and business need.
Assess and manage security risk before and during any supplier relationship.

controls.criteria: Addressing information security within supplier agreements
controls.notes: Bake security requirements into supplier contracts explicitly.

controls.criteria: Managing information security in the ICT supply chain
controls.notes: Extend security requirements down through your ICT supply chain, not just direct vendors.

controls.criteria: Monitoring, review and change management of supplier services
controls.notes: Regularly review supplier performance and security posture, and manage changes to their services.

controls.criteria: Information security for use of cloud services
controls.notes: Have a defined process for assessing and managing risk when adopting cloud services.

controls.criteria: Information security incident management planning and preparation
controls.notes: Have a documented incident management plan with clear roles before an incident happens.

controls.criteria: Assessment and decision on information security events
controls.notes: Have a defined process to triage security events and decide if they're incidents.
Follow documented response procedures when an incident is confirmed.
Conduct post-incident reviews and feed lessons learned back into controls.
Have procedures for identifying, collecting, and preserving evidence for incidents and investigations.
Maintain security controls, not just availability, during a disruption or crisis.
Ensure ICT systems and infrastructure can support business continuity requirements.

controls.criteria: Identification of legal, statutory, regulatory and contractual requirements
controls.notes: Maintain an up-to-date register of applicable legal, regulatory, and contractual security obligations.
Have procedures to protect intellectual property rights, yours and others', in how you use software and content.
Protect important records from loss, destruction, falsification, and unauthorized access, per retention requirements.
Identify and meet applicable privacy/PII protection requirements — ties closely to GDPR-style obligations.
Have your ISMS reviewed by an independent party (internal audit or external) at planned intervals.

controls.criteria: Compliance with policies and standards for information security
controls.notes: Regularly verify that teams are actually following the documented policies.
Document operating procedures for information processing facilities so they're consistent and repeatable.

categories.title: People Controls
categories.summary: How employees and contractors handle information day to day: screening, training, remote work, disciplinary process (8 controls).

controls.notes: Background-check candidates before hire, proportional to the role's access and risk level.
Include security responsibilities explicitly in employment contracts.

controls.criteria: Information security awareness, education and training
controls.notes: Provide regular, role-appropriate security training, and track completion.
Have a formal, documented disciplinary process for security policy violations.

controls.criteria: Responsibilities after termination or change of employment
controls.notes: Clearly communicate which security obligations survive termination or a role change.
Require signed NDAs reflecting the organization's actual confidentiality needs.
Have documented security requirements for remote/hybrid work (device security, network access, etc.).
Give employees a clear, easy way to report suspected security events, and encourage them to actually use it.

categories.title: Physical Controls
categories.summary: Protecting facilities, equipment, and media from physical and environmental threats (14 controls).

controls.notes: Define and protect physical security perimeters around areas containing sensitive information or assets.
Restrict and log physical entry to secure areas to authorized individuals only.
Apply appropriate physical security to offices, rooms, and facilities based on what they contain.
Continuously monitor premises for unauthorized physical access (cameras, alarms, guards).

controls.criteria: Protecting against physical and environmental threats
controls.notes: Design for protection against fire, flood, and other environmental or physical threats.
Define rules for how people work within secure areas, e.g. no unsupervised visitors.
Enforce clear-desk/clear-screen practices so sensitive information isn't left exposed.
Site and protect equipment to reduce risk from environmental threats and unauthorized access.
Apply equivalent security controls to assets used or stored outside company premises.
Manage storage media through its lifecycle (handling, transport, disposal) in line with classification.
Protect against failure of supporting utilities (power, HVAC) that could impact security or availability.
Protect power and data cabling from interception, interference, or damage.
Maintain equipment correctly to ensure its continued availability and integrity.

controls.criteria: Secure disposal or re-use of equipment
controls.notes: Verify all sensitive data and licensed software is removed before equipment is disposed of or reused.

categories.title: Technological Controls
categories.summary: The largest and most technical theme — endpoint security, access control, cryptography, secure development, network security, logging and monitoring (34 controls).

controls.notes: Protect information on user devices (laptops, phones) through a defined endpoint security policy.
Restrict and closely manage privileged/admin access separately from standard user access.
Restrict access to information and application functions per the access control policy.
Restrict and log access to source code, build tools, and related assets.
Implement secure authentication technologies (e.g. MFA) appropriate to the access being protected.
Monitor and plan resource capacity to meet current and expected future demand.
Implement malware detection and prevention combined with user awareness.
Identify, assess, and remediate technical vulnerabilities on a defined timeline.
Establish and monitor secure baseline configurations for hardware, software, and networks.
Securely delete information when it's no longer required, per retention policy.
Use data masking (or similar) to limit exposure of sensitive data, aligned to your access control and classification rules.
Apply measures to detect and prevent unauthorized disclosure of sensitive data.
Maintain and regularly test backups per a defined backup policy.
Build in enough redundancy to meet availability requirements.
Produce, store, and protect logs recording events, exceptions, and security-relevant activity.
Monitor systems and networks for anomalous behavior and respond to findings.
Synchronize clocks across systems to a single reference time source, so logs are correlatable.
Restrict and tightly control use of utility programs capable of overriding system or application controls.

controls.criteria: Installation of software on operational systems
controls.notes: Control software installation on production/operational systems through a formal process.
Secure, manage, and control networks to protect the information within them.
Identify and monitor the security mechanisms of network services, whether in-house or outsourced.
Segregate networks, groups of services, users, and systems to limit blast radius.
Manage access to external websites to reduce exposure to malicious content.
Define and apply a cryptography policy covering when and how encryption and key management are used.
Apply security requirements at every stage of the software development lifecycle.
Define and validate security requirements when developing or acquiring applications.

controls.criteria: Secure systems architecture and engineering principles
controls.notes: Apply secure-by-design engineering principles to system architecture.
Apply secure coding principles during software development.

controls.criteria: Security testing in development and acceptance
controls.notes: Test security as part of the development and acceptance process, not just before launch.
Direct, monitor, and review security for outsourced development work.

controls.criteria: Separation of development, test and production environments
controls.notes: Keep dev/test/production environments separate to reduce risk to production.
Control changes to information processing facilities and systems through a formal change management process.
Protect test data appropriately, especially if it's derived from production data.

controls.criteria: Protection of information systems during audit testing
controls.notes: Plan and agree audit/testing activities carefully to minimize disruption to production systems.
